summary: Gruber corroborates a TUAW report that Apple will seed developers with Mac OS X 10.6 at WWDC 2008, a release focused entirely on stability, security, and architectural improvements rather than new features. The release, codenamed 'Snow Leopard,' will be Intel-only and fully 64-bit, dropping PowerPC support. Gruber notes the name fits perfectly — like Leopard but refined for a specific environment. He expresses enthusiasm for a quality-focused release while acknowledging it seems premature to abandon PowerPC, especially G5 machines. He speculates on pricing, suggesting $129 is too much for architectural changes but a free update seems unlikely, guessing around $29. He also notes Apple is working to unify its various OS branches across Mac, iPhone, and Apple TV.
key_points: Mac OS X 10.6 'Snow Leopard' will focus on stability, security, and architectural improvements with no significant new features
The release marks a major platform shift: Intel-only and fully 64-bit, dropping PowerPC support entirely
A key effort is unifying the various branches of OS X across Mac, iPhone, and Apple TV
Traditional version numbering doesn't fit this kind of release — it's neither a minor bug fix nor a major feature update
Pricing is the big unknown, with $129 seeming too high for architectural changes and free seeming too optimistic
money_quotes: If you've ever wished that Apple would spend more time focusing on making existing parts of the OS work better rather than adding new features, this is going to be the release for you.
Like Leopard, but cooler, and adapted for a specific environment.
I wouldn't be surprised if Steve Jobs doesn't even mention the number 10.6, and refers to the OS only by name.
It doesn't sound right to charge $129 for an update whose changes are mostly (completely?) architectural.
A big part of the effort, from what I'm hearing, is unifying the various branches of OS X at Apple: Mac OS X, iPhone OS, Apple TV, etc.
I still think it seems too soon by at least a year to drop PowerPC support — especially for G5s, which are still extremely capable machines by today's standards — but that's the word on the street.
key_insight: Apple's decision to dedicate an entire OS release to architectural refinement rather than new features represents a mature acknowledgment that platform stability and unification across devices matters more than feature checklists.
